era_context: The Roman Empire is in the final stages of a civil war between Constantius II and the usurper Magnentius, which began in 350 CE. Magnentius controls the Western Empire (Gaul, Spain, Britain, North Africa), while Constantius II controls the East. The conflict is draining resources and military manpower from both sides. In the East, the Sassanid Persian Empire under Shapur II remains a persistent threat. Christianity continues to spread and consolidate its position as the empire's dominant religion.

events.title: Battle of Mons Seleucus (or Mursa Major): Constantius II defeats Magnentius
events.description: In a major battle (location and exact date debated by scholars), Constantius II's forces decisively defeat the usurper Magnentius. This victory is a turning point in the civil war, though Magnentius will continue to resist for another year before his final defeat and suicide. The battle is costly for both sides, with heavy casualties among the Roman legions. The victory demonstrates Constantius II's military capability and the loyalty of his generals, particularly Silvanus. The battle's significance lies not only in its immediate military outcome but in its role in determining the fate of the Western Roman Empire.
events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in multiple sources, though the exact date (353 vs. 354) and location are debated. Most modern scholars place it in 353 CE.
cross_references: Part of broader civil war (350–353 CE)

events.title: Athanasius of Alexandria exiled by Constantius II
events.description: Constantius II, who favors a semi-Arian theological position, exiles Athanasius, the Bishop of Alexandria, for the fifth time. Athanasius is one of the most prominent defenders of Nicene orthodoxy (the position that Christ is of the same substance as God the Father) and a fierce opponent of Arianism. His exile is a major victory for the Arian faction at court and represents the high point of imperial pressure against Nicene Christianity. Athanasius will spend much of the remainder of Constantius II's reign in exile, though he will be restored after Constantius's death in 361 CE.
events.certainty_note: Athanasius's exile in 353 CE is well-attested in multiple sources, including his own writings and later ecclesiastical histories.
cross_references: Part of broader Arian controversy (325–381 CE)

events.title: Gallus Caesar appointed to govern the East
events.description: Constantius II appoints his half-brother Gallus as Caesar (junior emperor) to govern the Eastern Empire while Constantius focuses on the civil war in the West. Gallus is given control of Syria and the eastern provinces, including responsibility for defending against Sassanid Persian incursions. However, Gallus proves to be an incompetent and brutal administrator, alienating the local population and the military. His tenure will be marked by mismanagement and conflict, and he will be recalled and executed in 354 CE after only a year in office.
events.certainty_note: Gallus's appointment as Caesar is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is sometimes given as 351 CE, but most modern scholars place it in 353 CE.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Mons Seleucus is disputed among modern scholars. Some sources place it in 353 CE, others in 354 CE. Additionally, the location of the battle is debated—some scholars argue it occurred near Mursa (modern Osijek, Croatia) rather than in Gaul. The significance of the battle as a 'decisive' turning point is also somewhat overstated in later sources; the civil war continued for another year after this battle, suggesting that the victory was not as conclusive as some accounts suggest. Regarding Gallus's appointment, some sources date it to 351 CE rather than 353 CE, creating uncertainty about the exact chronology.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 353 CE is moderately rich for the Roman Empire, particularly regarding political and military events. Ammianus Marcellinus is the most detailed source, but he is writing retrospectively and may have biases. Ecclesiastical sources provide detailed information about the Arian controversy and Athanasius's exile but are less concerned with political and military events. The record for the Sassanid Empire and regions outside the Mediterranean is sparse. The narrative is heavily shaped by court-sponsored histories and later Christian compilations, which may emphasize theological disputes over political and military realities.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Mons Seleucus leads to Magnentius's final defeat and suicide in 355
Athanasius's exile in 353 is reversed by his restoration under Julian in 361
Gallus's appointment leads to his recall and execution in 354
